quizzes.name: Romance Languages
quizzes.description: Distinguish between French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Italian.

questions.explanation: The word 'Onde' (where) and the use of 'fica' for location are characteristic of Portuguese.
Italian uses 'Dove' for where and 'si trova' (finds itself) for location.
Spanish uses the inverted question mark and 'dónde' with an accent.
The spelling 'bibliothèque' and the grave accent on 'Où' are unique to French here.
The use of 'vous' and the word 'pain' (bread) are distinctly French.

questions.text: Quisiera un poco de pan, por favor.
questions.explanation: 'Quisiera' is a common Spanish polite request, and 'pan' is the Spanish word for bread.

questions.text: Eu gostaria de um pouco de pão.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'ão' in 'pão' is a major phonetic marker for Portuguese.

questions.text: Vorrei un po' di pane, per favore.
questions.explanation: The conditional 'Vorrei' and the ending in 'pane' identify this as Italian.
'Aujourd'hui' is one of the most uniquely recognizable words in the French language.
The use of 'Hace' for weather and 'hoy' for today is standard Spanish.
Portuguese uses 'hoje' for today, which is similar but spelled differently than Spanish 'hoy'.
Italian uses 'oggi' for today and 'freddo' with a double 'd'.
The word 'mela' (apple) and 'mangio' (I eat) are Italian.
'Manzana' and 'roja' are classic Spanish vocabulary words.
The word 'maçã' with the cedilla and tilde is uniquely Portuguese.
'Je mange' and 'pomme' (apple) are French.

questions.text: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce soir ?
questions.explanation: The 'Qu'est-ce que' construction is a staple of French questioning.
Spanish uses 'noche' for night/evening and 'haces' for 'you do'.

questions.text: O que você vai fazer hoje à noite?
questions.explanation: 'Você' is the common second-person pronoun in Brazilian Portuguese.
'Stasera' is the Italian contraction for 'questa sera' (this evening).
'Arrivederci' is a globally recognized Italian farewell.
The tilde on the 'ñ' in 'mañana' is a key Spanish identifier.
Portuguese uses 'nh' instead of 'ñ' for the same sound.
French uses 'demain' for tomorrow and the 'À' preposition for 'until'.
The circumflex on 'fenêtre' (window) is very common in French spelling.
'Ventana' and 'abierta' are standard Spanish vocabulary.
Portuguese uses 'janela' for window, distinct from the other three.
The 'è' (is) with a grave accent is an Italian marker.
'Mi piace' is the Italian way to express liking something.
'Me gusta' is the Spanish equivalent of 'I like'.
Portuguese requires the preposition 'de' after 'gostar'.
The contraction 'J'aime' and the adverb 'beaucoup' are French.

questions.text: Il y a un chat dans le jardin.
questions.explanation: 'Il y a' means 'there is' in French; 'chat' is cat.

questions.text: Hay un gato en el jardín.
questions.explanation: 'Hay' is 'there is' in Spanish, and 'jardín' has the Spanish acute accent.
Portuguese uses 'Há' (there is) and 'no' (contraction of in + the).
Italian uses 'C'è' for 'there is' and 'nel' for 'in the'.
The hyphenated verb-pronoun inversion is typical of French questions.
'Usted' is the formal 'you' in Spanish; 'inglés' has the Spanish accent.
In Portuguese, 'inglês' is spelled with a circumflex accent.
Italian often omits the subject pronoun ('Lei') in this question.
The double 'n' and ending in 'ore' are common Italian traits.

questions.text: Siempre soñando con un futuro mejor.
questions.explanation: The 'ie' in 'Siempre' and the word 'mejor' are Spanish.

questions.text: Sempre sonhando com um futuro melhor.
questions.explanation: The 'nh' in 'sonhando' is a Portuguese phonetic marker.
'Toujours' and 'avenir' (future) are French.
'L'addition' is the specific French term for a restaurant bill.
'La cuenta' is the Spanish word for the bill.
The Portuguese 'A' as a feminine article is a key marker.
Italian uses 'Il' as the masculine article and 'conto' for the bill.
'C'est' and 'journée' (day) are French.
'Bella' and 'giornata' are Italian descriptors for a nice day.
'Hermoso' (beautiful) and 'día' (day) are Spanish.
Portuguese uses 'É' (is) with an acute accent and 'bonito' (beautiful).
The plural 'Quels' and 'projets' with a silent 's' are French.
Spanish uses 'Cuáles' for 'which ones' and 'planes' for plans.
'Quais são' is the Portuguese plural for 'which are'.
'Quali' and 'tuoi' are Italian plural markers.
'Merci' and 'aide' (help) are French.
'Muchas gracias' and 'ayuda' (help) are Spanish.
'Obrigado' and the contraction 'pela' (por + a) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Grazie mille per il tuo aiuto.
questions.explanation: 'Grazie mille' (thousand thanks) and 'aiuto' are Italian.

questions.text: Se eu tivesse mais tempo, eu viajaria pelo mundo.
questions.explanation: The imperfect subjunctive 'tivesse' and the conditional 'viajaria' are distinctively Portuguese.

questions.text: Si tuviera más tiempo, viajaría por el mundo.
questions.explanation: The Spanish imperfect subjunctive 'tuviera' and the use of 'por' for 'through' identify this.

questions.text: Se avessi più tempo, viaggerei per il mondo.
questions.explanation: The Italian double 's' in 'avessi' and the conditional ending 'ei' in 'viaggerei' are key.

questions.text: Si j'avais plus de temps, je voyagerais à travers le monde.
questions.explanation: The French 'avais' (imperfect) and the complex 'à travers le monde' are unique here.

questions.text: L'enfant joue avec un petit oiseau dans la forêt.
questions.explanation: The word 'oiseau' (bird) contains almost all French vowels and is a classic identifier.

questions.text: El niño juega con un pajarito en el bosque.
questions.explanation: 'Niño' (boy) and 'bosque' (forest) are standard Spanish vocabulary.

questions.text: O menino brinca com um passarinho na floresta.
questions.explanation: The word 'menino' and the 'fl' in 'floresta' are typical Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il bambino gioca con un uccellino nel bosco.
questions.explanation: The double consonants in 'bambino' and 'uccellino' are very characteristic of Italian.

questions.text: È necessario che tu faccia i compiti ogni giorno.
questions.explanation: The subjunctive 'faccia' and the word 'compiti' (homework) are Italian.

questions.text: Es necesario que hagas la tarea todos los días.
questions.explanation: The subjunctive 'hagas' and 'tarea' (homework) identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: É necessário que você faça os deveres todos os dias.
questions.explanation: The tilde in 'necessário' and 'faça' with a cedilla are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il est nécessaire que tu fasses tes devoirs chaque jour.
questions.explanation: The spelling of 'nécessaire' and the subjunctive 'fasses' are French.

questions.text: Acredito que eles chegarão amanhã de manhã.
questions.explanation: The future ending 'ão' in 'chegarão' is a major Portuguese giveaway.

questions.text: Creo que llegarán mañana por la mañana.
questions.explanation: The future ending 'án' and the word 'mañana' are Spanish.
The double 'r' in 'arriveranno' and 'mattina' are classic Italian.

questions.text: Je crois qu'ils arriveront demain matin.
questions.explanation: The French 'crois' and 'arriveront' identify this sentence.

questions.text: Voglio mangiare una pizza deliziosa in quel ristorante.
questions.explanation: The 'gl' sound in 'voglio' and the ending of 'ristorante' are Italian.

questions.text: Quero comer uma pizza deliciosa naquele restaurante.
questions.explanation: The contraction 'naquele' (em + aquele) is a specific Portuguese feature.

questions.text: Quiero comer una pizza deliciosa en ese restaurante.
questions.explanation: The 'ie' in 'Quiero' and the demonstrative 'ese' are Spanish.

questions.text: Je veux manger une pizza délicieuse dans ce restaurant.
questions.explanation: The French 'veux' and the spelling 'délicieuse' are unique identifiers.

questions.text: O cachorro está latindo para o gato preto.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'cachorro' for dog and the 'o' article before it.

questions.text: El perro está ladrando al gato negro.
questions.explanation: 'Perro' and the color 'negro' are standard Spanish.

questions.text: Le chien aboie après le chat noir.
questions.explanation: 'Chien' and 'noir' (black) are common French words.

questions.text: Il cane abbaia al gatto nero.
questions.explanation: The word 'cane' and 'gatto' are Italian markers here.

questions.text: Nous allons à la plage ce week-end.
questions.explanation: The French verb 'allons' and the noun 'plage' are distinct.

questions.text: Vamos a la playa este fin de semana.
questions.explanation: The word 'playa' (beach) and 'fin de semana' are Spanish.

questions.text: Andiamo in spiaggia questo fine settimana.
questions.explanation: The Italian 'andiamo' and 'spiaggia' (beach) with double 'g' are key.

questions.text: Nós vamos à praia este fim de semana.
questions.explanation: The word 'praia' and the grave accent on 'à' identify Portuguese.

questions.text: I miei genitori vivono in una grande città.
questions.explanation: The plural possessive 'I miei' and 'città' with the accent are Italian.

questions.text: Mis padres viven en una gran ciudad.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'padres' for parents and 'ciudad' for city.

questions.text: Meus pais vivem em uma cidade grande.
questions.explanation: 'Meus pais' and 'cidade' are common Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Mes parents vivent dans une grande ville.
questions.explanation: 'Mes parents' and 'ville' (city) are French terms.

questions.text: Elle lit un livre intéressant dans sa chambre.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'Elle' and the word 'livre' are French.

questions.text: Ella lee un libro interesante en su habitación.
questions.explanation: The double 'e' in 'lee' (reads) and 'habitación' are Spanish.

questions.text: Lei legge un libro interessante nella sua camera.
questions.explanation: The double 'g' in 'legge' and the pronoun 'Lei' are Italian.

questions.text: Ela lê um livro interessante no seu quarto.
questions.explanation: The circumflex in 'lê' and the word 'quarto' (room) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Temos que comprar leite e ovos no mercado.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'em' and words like 'leite' and 'ovos' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Tenemos que comprar leche y huevos en el mercado.
questions.explanation: 'Leche' and 'huevos' (eggs) are standard Spanish.

questions.text: Dobbiamo comprare latte e uova al mercato.
questions.explanation: The double 'b' in 'dobbiamo' and 'latte' (milk) are Italian.

questions.text: Nous devons acheter du lait et des œufs au marché.
questions.explanation: The French partitive articles 'du' and 'des' identify this sentence.

questions.text: L'eau est très froide ce matin.
questions.explanation: The word 'eau' (water) is a classic French vowel-heavy word.

questions.text: L'acqua è molto fredda questa mattina.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'acqua' with 'cq' is unique to Italian.

questions.text: El agua está muy fría esta mañana.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'agua' and the feminine 'esta mañana'.

questions.text: A água está muito fria esta manhã.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'A' as the article for 'água'.

questions.text: Per favore, chiuda la porta quando esce.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'chiuda' (close) and 'porta' are Italian.

questions.text: Por favor, cierre la puerta cuando salga.
questions.explanation: The Spanish 'ie' in 'cierre' and the subjunctive 'salga' are key.

questions.text: Por favor, feche a porta quando sair.
questions.explanation: The word 'feche' (close) is unique to Portuguese among these.

questions.text: S'il vous plaît, fermez la porte quand vous sortez.
questions.explanation: The verb 'fermez' and 'sortez' identify this as French.

questions.text: Cette voiture rouge est très rapide.
questions.explanation: The word 'voiture' (car) is a common French term.

questions.text: Este coche rojo es muy rápido.
questions.explanation: The word 'coche' and the color 'rojo' are Spanish identifiers.

questions.text: Este carro vermelho é muito rápido.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'carro' and the color 'vermelho'.

questions.text: Questa macchina rossa è molto veloce.
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'macchina' for car and 'rossa' for the color red.

questions.text: Il pleut toujours en automne ici.
questions.explanation: 'Il pleut' (it rains) and 'automne' are French.
The double 'l' in 'Llueve' and 'otoño' with the 'ñ' are Spanish.
The 'ch' in 'Chove' and 'outono' are Portuguese identifiers.
The word 'piove' (it rains) and 'autunno' are Italian.

questions.text: Vorrei un bicchiere di vino rosso.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'bicchiere' (glass) is a classic Italian identifier.

questions.text: Quisiera una copa de vino tinto.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'copa' for wine glass and 'tinto' for red wine.

questions.text: Eu gostaria de um copo de vinho tinto.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'copo' (which looks like Spanish 'copa' but is masculine).

questions.text: Je voudrais un verre de vin rouge.
questions.explanation: The French 'verre' (glass) and 'vin' are distinct here.

quizzes.name: Slavic Languages
quizzes.description: Distinguish between Russian, Ukrainian, and Belarusian

questions.explanation: Russian uses the Cyrillic script exclusively and has distinctive grammar patterns.
Ukrainian often uses ї and є which are rare or absent in Russian. The spelling pattern is distinctive.
Belarusian uses ў (u-breve) and has characteristic spelling and grammar that differ from Russian and Ukrainian.
This common Russian greeting uses the Russian word order and grammar patterns.
The Ukrainian 'як' (how) and 'у' (in) structure are distinctly Ukrainian.
The Belarusian word 'справы' and the characteristic spelling pattern mark this as Belarusian.
The Russian adjective ending 'ую' and the distinctive word forms are characteristic.
The Ukrainian adjective 'українську' with its distinctive ending marks this as Ukrainian.
The Belarusian 'беларускую' and the spelling 'літаратуру' (with 'і') are distinctly Belarusian.
Russian uses 'это' and the adjective ending 'ый' is typical morphology.
Ukrainian 'це', 'дуже' (very) and 'місто' (city) are distinctive.
Belarusian 'гэта', 'вельмі' and 'прыгожы' (beautiful) are distinctly Belarusian.
Russian uses 'мне нужна', a distinctive construction.
Ukrainian 'мені' and 'потрібна', along with 'допомога', are distinctly Ukrainian.
Belarusian 'мне' and 'трэба' (need) are distinctly Belarusian.
The Russian word 'сейчас' (now) is characteristic.
Ukrainian 'зараз' and the use of 'і' in 'офісі' are Ukrainian.
Belarusian 'працую' and the ў (u-breve) before 'офісе' are distinctive.
'Вчера' (yesterday) and 'хорошая' are typical Russian.
Ukrainian 'вчора' and 'гарна' (good/beautiful) are distinctive.
The word 'надвор'е' (weather) and 'учора' are Belarusian.
The Russian greeting 'Доброе утро' is standard.
Ukrainian uses 'Доброго ранку' and the vocative-like 'друзі'.
'Добрай раніцы' and 'сябры' (friends) are uniquely Belarusian.
Standard Russian phrasing for 'What is your name?'.
Ukrainian uses 'звати' (to call) in this context.
Belarusian uses 'завуць' with the characteristic 'ць' ending.
Russian 'пожалуйста' (please) is very distinctive.
Ukrainian 'будь ласка' and 'хвилину' (minute) are distinctive.
Belarusian 'калі ласка' and 'хвіліну' (with 'і') mark this language.
Russian 'понимаю' and 'этот' (this) are standard.
Ukrainian 'розумію' and 'питання' (question) are key identifiers.
Belarusian 'разумею' and 'пытанне' (with 'ы') are distinctive.
Russian uses 'где' and the verb 'живёте' with 'ё'.
Ukrainian 'де' and 'живете' (with 'и') are characteristic.
Belarusian 'дзе' and 'жывяце' (with 'я') are distinctive.
Russian 'спасибо' (thank you) is the most common form.
Ukrainian 'дякую' and 'подарунок' are distinctive.
Belarusian 'дзякуй' and 'падарунак' (with 'а') are characteristic.
Russian 'книга' and 'столе' (prepositional case) are standard.
Ukrainian uses 'столі' (with 'і') in the locative case.
Belarusian 'кніга' (with 'і') and 'ляжыць' are distinctive.
Russian 'говорите' and the prefix 'по-' are typical.
Ukrainian 'розмовляєте' (to speak) is uniquely Ukrainian.
Belarusian 'размаўляеце' with 'ў' is characteristic.
Russian 'сегодня' (today) and 'холодный' are standard.
Ukrainian 'сьогодні' (with 'і') is the distinctive form.
Belarusian 'сёння' and 'дзень' (with 'дз') are characteristic.
Standard Russian wish before a meal.
Ukrainian uses the short and unique word 'смачного'.
Belarusian 'смачна есці' is the traditional greeting.
Russian 'хочу' (want) and 'пить' (drink) are standard.
Ukrainian 'пити' (with 'и') is the infinitive form.
Belarusian 'хачу' (with 'а') and 'піць' (with 'і') are distinctive.
Russian 'до свидания' is the formal goodbye.
Ukrainian 'побачення' and 'зустрічі' are uniquely Ukrainian.
Belarusian 'пабачэння' (with 'а') and 'сустрэчы' are characteristic.
Russian 'учится' and 'университете' (with 'е') are typical.
Ukrainian 'мій', 'навчається' and 'університеті' (with 'і') are distinctive.
Belarusian 'вучыцца' and the use of 'ў' are unique.

quizzes.name: Scandinavian Languages
quizzes.description: Distinguish between Norwegian (Bokmål), Swedish, and Danish.

questions.explanation: Norwegian uses 'jeg' (like Danish) and 'eple' with a 'p'. It also uses the 'ø' and 'æ' characters.
Danish uses 'æble' with a 'b', whereas Norwegian uses a 'p'.
Swedish is easily identified by the use of 'ä' and 'ö' instead of 'æ' and 'ø'.
The 'hv-' start for question words and 'd' in 'hedder' are characteristic of Danish.
Norwegian drops the 'd' in 'hva' and uses 't' in 'heter' where Danish uses 'd'.
Swedish uses 'Vad' and 'heter' (with a 't') along with its distinct vowel markings.
Uses 'hvor' (where) and the compound 'togstasjonen' with a 'g'.
Danish uses 'stationen' (with a 't') rather than the Norwegian 'stasjonen'.
Swedish uses 'Var' for 'Where' and the 'å' character in 'tåg'.
The word 'flicka' (girl) and 'och' (and) are uniquely Swedish in this group.
Norwegian uses 'gutt' (boy) and 'jente' (girl), and 'og' for 'and'.
Danish uses 'dreng' (boy) and 'pige' (girl).
Uses 'vår' (our) and 'butikk' with a double 'k'.
Swedish uses 'Välkommen' with an 'ä' and a single 'k' in 'butik'.
Danish uses 'vores' for 'our' and a single 'k' in 'butik'.
The 'ö' and 'ä' characters are the primary giveaway for Swedish.
Uses 'ø' and the verb 'gjør' (makes/does).
Danish uses 'gør', which is spelled with a 'ø' but lacks the 'j' found in Norwegian 'gjør'.
Swedish uses 'många' (many) and 'ä' in 'bäckar'.
Danish uses 'mange' and 'bække'.
Norwegian uses 'mange' and 'bekker' with a 'k' where Danish uses 'k'.
Swedish uses 'kärleken' (the love) with an 'ä'.
Norwegian uses 'kjærlighet' with a 'j' and 'æ'.
Danish uses 'kærlighed' with a 'd' ending where Norwegian uses 't'.

questions.text: Det er deilig å være norsk.
questions.explanation: Though this is a famous slogan about Norway, 'deilig' and the syntax are classically Danish.

questions.text: Det er deilig å være norsk.
questions.explanation: In written form (Bokmål), this sentence is identical to Danish, but often associated with Norwegian tourism.

questions.text: Det är härligt att vara svensk.
questions.explanation: Uses 'är' instead of 'er' and 'att' instead of 'å'.
Uses 'viktigste' (most important) and 'frokost' (breakfast).
Danish uses 'morgenmad' for breakfast, whereas in Norwegian 'frokost' is breakfast.
Swedish uses 'frukost' (u instead of o) and 'är'.

quizzes.name: Northern Neighbors
quizzes.description: Distinguish between Swedish, Finnish, and Estonian

questions.explanation: Swedish is a Germanic language. Words like 'var' (where) and 'ligger' (lies/is located) are cognates with English and German.
Finnish uses long double vowels ('lähin') and specific suffixes. It is a Finno-Ugric language, distinct from Swedish.
Estonian is closely related to Finnish but often 'drops' the final vowel seen in Finnish words (peatus vs pysäkki).
Uses the characteristic Germanic 'jag' (I) and the verb 'pratar' (speak).
Finnish negation uses a conjugated verb 'en' followed by the main verb.
Estonian uses 'ma' for 'I' and 'ei' as a universal negation particle, unlike the conjugated Finnish version.
Swedish uses 'tack' for please/thank you, similar to the Norwegian and Danish 'tak'.
Finnish often ends loanwords with an 'i' (Kuppi) and uses 'kiitos' for thanks.
Estonian uses 'ü' (like German) and 'palun' for please.
The 'Välkommen' is a classic North Germanic greeting.
The 'Terve-' prefix is common in Finnish greetings.
Estonian 'Tere' is shorter than the Finnish 'Tervetuloa'.
Swedish adds '-et' to the end of nouns (Huset) to indicate 'the'.
Finnish colors often end in '-inen'.
Estonian words are often shorter versions of their Finnish cousins (punane vs punainen).
A standard Germanic question structure meaning 'What is your name?'.
Uses the possessive suffix '-si' on the word name (nimi).
Uses 'mis' for what and 'su' as a short form of your.
Features the Swedish 'idag' (today) and Germanic 'snöar' (snowing).
Finnish uses 'lunta' (partitive of lumi) to describe the snow falling.
Note the word 'täna' (today), which lacks the double vowels of Finnish 'tänään'.
Swedish 'eller' (or) and 'kött' (meat) are very common vocabulary words.
The suffix '-kö' turns the verb 'syöt' into a question.

questions.text: Kas sa sööd liha või kala?
questions.explanation: Estonian uses the particle 'Kas' at the start of a sentence to form a question.
Swedish days of the week end in '-dag'.
Finnish days are borrowed from Germanic but given a distinct vowel-heavy spelling.
Estonian days end in '-päev' (day).
The letter 'ö' at the start of a word is common in Swedish commands.
Uses 'ikkuna' for window, a loan from Russian but adapted to Finnish phonology.
The Estonian word 'aken' is related to Finnish 'ikkuna' but significantly shortened.

quizzes.name: Northern Indic Scripts
quizzes.description: Distinguish between Devanagari, Bengali-Assamese, and Gurmukhi scripts.

questions.explanation: Standard Devanagari script. The word 'क्या' (what) is a classic Hindi marker.
Uses the Bengali-Assamese script. Note the triangular 'ব' and the specific shape of the 'ki' (কি).
Uses Gurmukhi script. Characterized by more squared-off letters and the unique 'ਤ' (ta).
Devanagari script, but uses the retroflex 'ळ' (la) which is a hallmark of Marathi among the major northern languages.
Devanagari script. The ending 'के हो' (ke ho) is the standard question marker in Nepali.
Identifiable by the character 'ৰ' (ra) with a diagonal stroke, which differs from Bengali's 'র'.
Classic Devanagari with the 'm' sound represented by the anusvara (dot) over the 'ह'.
Written in Devanagari; the nasalized 'तें' is very common in Goan Konkani.
Uses the Bengali script (traditionally), but the vocabulary and sentence structure are Tibeto-Burman.
Uses Gurmukhi. The verb ending 'ਹਾਂ' (ha) is very distinct.
Devanagari script. The word 'खूप' (very) is a common Marathi vocabulary marker.
Bengali script. 'আমি' (I) and 'খাই' (eat) are fundamental Bengali words.
Devanagari with visarga (colon-like dots) at the end of words, a primary feature of Sanskrit.
Gurmukhi script. This is the standard Sikh greeting.
Devanagari script. 'मलाई' (to me) is a typical Nepali grammatical construction.
Devanagari. Uses the Persian-influenced 'z' (ज़) with a dot (nuqta), common in Hindi.
Uses the unique Assamese 'ৱ' or 'ৰ' and the first-person pronoun 'মই' (Moi).
Devanagari. The use of 'स' and 'त' endings is common in Konkani syntax.
Bengali script used for Meiteilon (Manipuri). Distinctive spacing and syllable structure.
Gurmukhi script. The 'u' vowel marker in 'ਤੁਸੀਂ' is placed uniquely below the letter.
Devanagari. The 'li' (ली) feminine verb ending is a Marathi marker.
Bengali script. Note the 'sho' sound represented by 'স' (though often pronounced 'sh').
Devanagari. 'भवान्' (you - masculine) is a classic Sanskrit formal address.
Devanagari. 'वहाँ' (there) and 'कौन' (who) are basic Hindi identifiers.
Bengali-Assamese script. Uses the apostrophe-like 'over-dot' common in Assamese transliteration.
Devanagari. 'खूब बरें' (Very good) is a common Goan expression.
Devanagari. 'भेटौंला' (will meet) uses the unique Nepali nasal 'o' marker.
Gurmukhi script. 'ਕਿਵੇਂ' (how) is a specific Punjabi interrogative.
Bengali-Assamese script. Note the specific 'r' sound in 'বৰষুণ' (rain).
Bengali script. The verb 'ভালোবাসি' (love) is iconic to the Bengali language.
Devanagari. The word 'बसा' (sit) is distinctively Marathi.
Devanagari. The 'आसात' ending is a common verb form in Konkani.
Gurmukhi script. 'ਸਾਡਾ' (our) is a key Punjabi word.
Standard Hindi Devanagari using the plural marker 'हैं'.
Assamese script. The verb ending 'খালোঁ' is specific to Assamese past tense.
Classical Sanskrit in Devanagari with characteristic dative case endings.
Devanagari script. 'कहाँ छ' is the standard way to ask 'where is' in Nepali.

questions.text: আপনার সাথে দেখা করে ভালো লাগলো।
questions.explanation: Bengali script. A formal phrase meaning 'Nice to meet you'.
Gurmukhi script. Uses the 'adak' (symbol over letters) to indicate double consonants.
Marathi Devanagari. 'हवा' (want) is a common Marathi modal verb.
Devanagari. 'सांगितली' (told) shows the Marathi past tense feminine agreement.
Bengali script. High-register vocabulary used in formal Bengali.
Sanskrit for 'I do not know', using Devanagari.
Hindi Devanagari. 'ठंड' (cold) is a common vocabulary word.
Gurmukhi script. 'ਕਿੱਥੇ' (where) is the Punjabi interrogative.
Devanagari script. 'मनपर्छ' (like) is written as one word in Nepali.
Assamese script. 'ধুনীয়া' (beautiful) is a classic Assamese word.
Konkani Devanagari. 'हाव' (I) and 'केंन्ना' (when) are specific to the language.
Bengali script. A common Bengali proverb about education.
Gurmukhi script. Uses unique vowel placements above the letters.
Basic Sanskrit verb conjugations in Devanagari.
Marathi Devanagari. The optative 'जावो' (let it go/be) is typical for wishes.
Devanagari script. 'माया लाग्छ' is the common way to express affection in Nepali.

questions.text: ਕੀ ਤੁਸੀਂ ਮੇਰੀ ਮਦਦ ਕਰ ਸਕਦੇ ਹੋ?
questions.explanation: Gurmukhi script. 'ਮਦਦ' (help) is a common loanword used in Punjabi.
Bengali script. 'করছো' (doing) shows the present continuous in Bengali.
Assamese script. Mentions 'Assam' (অসম) using the unique character 'স'.
The national motto of India, written in Devanagari script.
Devanagari script. 'हेर्न जान्छु' (going to see) is a Nepali verbal phrase.
Marathi Devanagari. 'करतो आहेस' (are doing) is the standard present continuous for 'you'.
Simple Hindi sentence in Devanagari script.

quizzes.name: Southern & SE Asian Scripts
quizzes.description: Master the art of distinguishing between the rounded, curly, and ornate scripts of South and Southeast Asia.

questions.explanation: Extremely dense and circular; lacks the top 'ticks' seen in Telugu or Kannada.
Distinctly 'coiled' look. It lacks the horizontal lines of Hindi and is more 'bubbly' than Tamil.
Look for the distinct 'check-mark' or 'tick' (talakattu) at the top of the characters.
Virtually identical to Telugu but often features slightly more complex internal loops.
Defined by the large 'umbrella' or curved cap over almost every letter.
The 'roundest' script. Composed almost entirely of overlapping circles.
Notice the small 'heads' (tiny circles) at the start of the strokes and its vertical, fluid look.
Similar to Thai but more rounded/loopy with fewer sharp angles.

questions.text: តើអ្នកសុខសប្បាយជាទេ?
questions.explanation: Highly ornate and jagged. It looks heavier and more 'ancient' than Thai.
A traditional script with a very wavy, consistent 'mountain-peak' flow.
The word for 'Thanks'. Very circular and lacks top-side adornments.
Note the intricate 'y' shapes at the end which are unique to Sri Lankan Sinhala.
The check-marks are clearly visible on the first and third characters.
Note the blockier nature of some loops compared to Telugu.
The 'umbrella' curves are the dead giveaway here.

questions.text: ကျေးဇူးတင်ပါတယ်။
questions.explanation: Pure circularity. No straight lines or sharp corners.
The small loops at the base of the letters are characteristic of Thai.
More simplified and rounded than its Thai counterpart.
Deeply intricate with small 'tails' on top of characters.
Consistent zigzag/wavy pattern across the baseline.
The 'm' sound at the end is a small circle, common in Malayalam.
The famous Sri Lankan greeting; note the 'coiled' aesthetic.
Similar to Malayalam but uses the top tick mark.
Features the top tick but lacks the final circular 'm' found in Telugu/Malayalam versions.
The first character is encased in the signature Odia round cap.
Looks like a series of interconnected zeros or 'o's.

questions.text: ยินดีต้อนรับ
questions.explanation: Features many vertical stalks with small 'heads'.

questions.text: ຍິນດີຕ້ອນຮັບ
questions.explanation: Notice the softer, more cursive flow compared to Thai.

questions.text: សូមស្វាគមន៍
questions.explanation: The most complex and 'busy' looking script in this group.
The script looks like a continuous wave across the page.
No top ticks; extremely compact and round.
The vowel markers on the left of characters are very 'curly'.
Classic Telugu structure with the 'check' marks on top.
Round, but the 'tick' is slightly more angled than Telugu.
The 'umbrella' shape is dominant throughout.
Very little variation in stroke height; mostly circles.
The first character is a tall, thin vowel marker with a head at the bottom.
The 'u' sound marker (bottom) is very loopy.
Distinctive 'roofs' on characters that are more jagged than Odia.
Distinctive tall markers at the start and end of segments.
Round, but contains the 'ksh' conjunct which is very dense.
The left-side vowel marker is a single, large loop.
Notice the 'm' (circle with a tick) at the end.
Lacks the final circular 'm' used in Telugu for this word.
The final character has a unique 'y' hook under the umbrella.
The stacking of circles is very common in Burmese words.
Relatively few 'heads' in this specific word, but very vertical.
The 'n' at the end (last char) is more curved than Thai.
The first letter has a double-leg look common in Khmer.
Consistent rhythm and height across the word.
The word for 'water'. Very loopy and finishes with a circle.
Each character is almost a self-contained coil.
The long 'ii' vowel marker is a curl on top of the first letter.
Similar to Telugu, but the 'u' marker on the second letter is distinct.
Two very prominent round caps.
Only two characters, both based on the circle.
The small circle above the second character is a vowel marker.
Similar to Thai but with more 'swooping' lines.
The 'k' at the end is very jagged and ornate.
A very short, wavy example of the script.

quizzes.name: Malay vs. Indonesian
quizzes.description: Distinguish between the two major standardized registers of the Malay language.

questions.text: Saya mahu memesan teksi ke lapangan terbang.
questions.explanation: Malay often uses 'mahu' for 'want' (though also used in ID, it is the standard preference here) and 'lapangan terbang' is the standard term for airport.

questions.text: Saya ingin memesan taksi ke bandara.
questions.explanation: Indonesian uses 'taksi' (Dutch influence) and 'bandara' (abbreviation of bandar udara) for airport.
In Malay, 'kereta' means car. In Indonesian, 'kereta' usually refers to a train (kereta api).
Indonesian uses 'mobil' (from automobile) for car, whereas Malay uses 'kereta'.
Malay uses 'bil' (English loan) and 'sila' for please in this context.
Indonesian uses 'bon' (Dutch loan) for a bill or receipt.
'Sila' is the standard polite 'please' in Malaysia for invitations or instructions.
Indonesian uses 'antre' (Dutch 'antrie') for queueing, while Malay uses 'beratur'.
Both use 'rumah sakit' (hospital), but in this specific quiz context, Indonesian uses this exclusively while Malay often uses 'hospital'.
Malay has adopted 'hospital' from English as the primary formal term.

questions.text: Pejabat pos terletak di hujung jalan.
questions.explanation: Malay uses 'pejabat' for office.

questions.text: Kantor pos terletak di ujung jalan.
questions.explanation: Indonesian uses 'kantor' (Dutch 'kantoor') for office.

questions.text: Saya akan pergi ke kedai runcit.
questions.explanation: 'Kedai' is the standard Malay word for shop.

questions.text: Saya akan pergi ke toko kelontong.
questions.explanation: Indonesian uses 'toko' for shop and 'kelontong' for a small grocery store.
Malay uses 'syarikat' (Arabic origin) for company.
Indonesian uses 'perusahaan' for company and 'sukses' (Dutch 'succes') for successful.
While both understand this, the suffix '-kah' is a classic formal Malay interrogative marker.
Indonesian frequently uses the '-nya' suffix as a definite article or possessive marker in daily speech.

questions.text: Tandas lelaki ada di sebelah kiri.
questions.explanation: Malay uses 'tandas' for toilet.

questions.text: Kamar kecil pria ada di sebelah kiri.
questions.explanation: Indonesian uses 'kamar kecil' or 'WC' and 'pria' for male.

questions.text: Bas itu lewat dua puluh minit.
questions.explanation: Malay uses 'bas' (English) and 'minit' (English).

questions.text: Bis itu terlambat dua puluh menit.
questions.explanation: Indonesian uses 'bis' and 'menit' (Dutch 'minuut').
Malay uses 'kerani' (clerk).
Indonesian tends toward modern loanwords like 'staf' or 'pegawai'.

questions.text: Kuala Lumpur ialah ibu negara Malaysia.
questions.explanation: Malay uses 'ibu negara' for capital city.
Indonesian uses 'ibu kota' for capital city.
Malay uses 'hari lahir' for birthday.
Indonesian uses 'ulang tahun' for birthdays and anniversaries.

questions.text: Keluarga saya tinggal di rumah pangsa.
questions.explanation: Malay uses 'rumah pangsa' for flat/apartment.
Indonesian uses the loanword 'apartemen'.
